agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"Order cancellation feature for an order management system. Three stakeholders (Product, Finance, Operations) have provided requirements that, when composed, create a logical impossibility: orders are invoiced at placement, invoiced orders cannot be cancelled, yet orders should be cancellable before shipment.","bounded_context":"Order Management / Order Lifecycle","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Order","definition":"A commercial transaction record representing a customer's purchase, progressing through a lifecycle of states from placement to shipment and beyond."},{"term":"Cancellation","definition":"The act of voiding an order, reversing its commercial effect. Subject to lifecycle constraints defined by business rules."},{"term":"Invoice","definition":"A financial document issued against an order that locks in pricing and creates an accounts-receivable obligation. Described by Finance as irreversible."},{"term":"Shipment","definition":"The physical fulfillment step where goods leave the warehouse. Represents a point-of-no-return for the Product team's cancellation window."},{"term":"Order Placement","definition":"The initial act of creating an order. Per Operations, this is also the moment invoicing occurs."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"An order may be cancelled at any time before it ships.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is a business policy defining a cancellation window, not a data-integrity constraint. A VP could change the window (e.g., allow cancellation up to 1 hour before shipment).","testable_assertion":"Given an order in any pre-shipment state, when a cancellation request is submitted, then the order transitions to Cancelled."},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"An order that has been invoiced cannot be cancelled; invoicing is irreversible.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Finance treats invoicing as creating an irreversible financial obligation. Violating this would corrupt the financial ledger — reversing an invoice requires a credit note, not a cancellation.","testable_assertion":"Given an order that has an associated invoice, when a cancellation request is submitted, then the request is rejected."},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"Orders are invoiced at the time of placement to lock in pricing.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is an operational policy decision about when to invoice. The timing could be changed (e.g., invoice at shipment) without corrupting system state.","testable_assertion":"Given a new order is placed, when placement completes, then an invoice is immediately generated and associated with the order."}],"acceptance_criteria":[{"id":"AC-1","given":"An order has been placed but not yet shipped","when":"A cancellation request is submitted","then":"Per BR-1 the order should be cancelled, but per BR-2 + BR-3 the order is already invoiced and cancellation must be rejected — CONTRADICTION"},{"id":"AC-2","given":"An order has been placed and an invoice exists","when":"A cancellation request is submitted","then":"The system rejects the cancellation because the invoice is irreversible (BR-2)"},{"id":"AC-3","given":"An order has shipped","when":"A cancellation request is submitted","then":"The system rejects the cancellation because the shipment window has passed (BR-1)"},{"id":"AC-4","given":"A new order is being placed","when":"Order placement completes","then":"An invoice is generated and associated with the order immediately (BR-3)"}],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"Customer attempts to cancel an order seconds after placement","expected_behavior":"UNRESOLVABLE: BR-1 says allow (not yet shipped), BR-2+BR-3 say reject (already invoiced at placement). This is the core contradiction."},{"scenario":"Partial shipment — some items shipped, some not","expected_behavior":"Undefined. Even if partial cancellation were considered, invoicing at placement still blocks any cancellation under BR-2."},{"scenario":"Invoice generation fails or is delayed during placement","expected_behavior":"If the invoice hasn't been created yet, BR-2 would not block cancellation. However, this contradicts BR-3 which requires invoicing at placement. An async failure could create an inconsistent window."},{"scenario":"Credit note or refund issued instead of cancellation","expected_behavior":"Finance distinguishes cancellation from credit notes. A credit note reverses the financial effect without 'cancelling' the invoice, potentially resolving the contradiction — but this was not specified."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"If BR-2+BR-3 take precedence, customers can never cancel orders, leading to increased chargebacks, refund disputes, and potential regulatory issues (consumer protection laws often mandate cancellation windows).","operational":"Operations invoices at placement to lock pricing. If cancellation is allowed, they need a process for voiding or crediting invoices, which contradicts Finance's irreversibility stance. Resolution will require a new workflow (e.g., credit notes).","user":"Customers expect to cancel orders before shipment (industry standard). A system that prevents all cancellations will generate significant support volume, negative reviews, and churn.","risk":"High risk of regulatory non-compliance. Many jurisdictions (EU Consumer Rights Directive, US state laws) mandate cooling-off periods where cancellation must be permitted regardless of invoicing status.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Customers","impact":"Cannot cancel any order under current rules, violating reasonable expectations and potentially consumer protection rights."},{"group":"Customer Support","impact":"Will receive high volume of cancellation requests they cannot fulfill, leading to escalations and chargebacks."},{"group":"Finance Team","impact":"Must reconcile their irreversibility requirement with the business need to allow cancellations. Will need to define a credit-note or invoice-voiding process."},{"group":"Operations Team","impact":"May need to change invoicing timing (e.g., invoice at shipment instead of placement) or introduce a 'proforma invoice' concept that is reversible."},{"group":"Product Management","impact":"Core feature requirement (cancel before ship) is unimplementable under current finance/ops constraints. Must negotiate resolution."}]},"confidence":{"level":45,"reasoning":"The spec contains an explicit three-way contradiction that makes the cancellation feature unimplementable as stated. All three requirements are clearly stated, but they are mutually incompatible. No amount of interpretation resolves this — stakeholder alignment is required before implementation can proceed.","high_confidence_areas":["The contradiction is real and not a matter of interpretation","BR-2 (invoice irreversibility) is correctly classified as an invariant","BR-3 (invoice at placement) + BR-2 (no cancel if invoiced) = no cancellation ever possible","The domain language (order, invoice, shipment, cancellation) is standard and unambiguous"],"low_confidence_areas":["Whether Finance would accept a credit-note workflow as distinct from cancellation","Whether Operations would accept delaying invoicing to post-shipment or using proforma invoices","Regulatory requirements for cancellation windows in the target market"],"assumptions":["All three stakeholder requirements are accurately stated and currently enforced","There is no existing credit-note or invoice-voiding process that Finance considers distinct from cancellation","The system does not already have a workaround for this conflict"]},"escalations":[{"type":"contradiction","description":"BR-1 (cancel before shipment) is impossible to satisfy because BR-3 (invoice at placement) combined with BR-2 (invoiced orders cannot be cancelled) means every order is non-cancellable from the moment it is created. The cancellation feature as specified by the PM can never execute successfully.","affected_stakeholders":["Product Management","Finance Team","Operations Team","Customers","Customer Support"],"options":["A: Change invoicing timing — Operations invoices at shipment instead of placement, opening a cancellation window between placement and shipment. Requires Operations to find another mechanism to lock pricing (e.g., price snapshots).","B: Introduce credit notes — Finance distinguishes between 'cancelling an invoice' (forbidden) and 'issuing a credit note against an invoice' (allowed). Cancellation triggers a credit note rather than voiding the invoice. Invoice remains on the ledger but is financially neutralized.","C: Introduce proforma invoices — Operations issues a non-binding proforma at placement for price-locking. The legal invoice is generated at shipment. Proforma can be voided freely, enabling cancellation.","D: Restrict PM's requirement — Accept that cancellation is only possible in the brief window before invoicing completes (effectively never, given synchronous invoicing at placement)."],"recommendation":"Option B (credit notes) is the industry-standard resolution. It preserves Finance's ledger integrity (invoices are never deleted/voided), satisfies Operations' price-locking need (invoice is issued at placement), and enables the PM's cancellation feature (credit note reverses the financial effect). This is how most ERP and order management systems handle this exact conflict."}],"rejection_reasons":[{"violation":"The three requirements as stated are mutually exclusive and cannot be implemented together.","business_rule_broken":"BR-1 is rendered impossible by the combination of BR-2 and BR-3. Every order is invoiced at creation (BR-3), and invoiced orders cannot be cancelled (BR-2), therefore no order can ever be cancelled — directly contradicting BR-1."}]}
agent_output_excerpt: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"Order cancellation feature for an order management system. Three stakeholders (Product, Finance, Operations) have provided requirements that, when composed, create a logical impossibility: orders are invoiced at placement, invoiced orders cannot be cancelled, yet orders should be cancellable before shipment.","bounded_context":"Order Management / Order Lifecycle","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Order","definition":"A commercial transaction record represent

content.thinking:
Let me analyze these three requirements:

1. PM: "An order can be cancelled at any time before it ships."
2. Finance: "Orders that have been invoiced cannot be cancelled — invoicing is irreversible."
3. Operations: "We invoice orders at the time of placement to lock in pricing."

This is a clear contradiction:
- PM says cancellation is allowed before shipping
- Finance says invoiced orders cannot be cancelled
- Operations says orders are invoiced at placement time

Combined: orders are invoiced at placement → invoiced orders can't be cancelled → orders can NEVER be cancelled. 

This directly conflicts with the PM's requirement that cancellation is possible before shipping. I'm flagging this as a contradiction with low confidence since the teams clearly haven't aligned on their core assumptions about when invoicing happens.

Will need to define a credit-note or invoice-voiding process."},{"group":"Operations Team","impact":"May need to change invoicing timing (e.g., invoice at shipment instead of placement) or introduce a 'proforma invoice' concept that is reversible."},{"group":"Product Management","impact":"Core feature requirement (cancel before ship) is unimplementable under current finance/ops constraints. The cancellation feature as specified by the PM can never execute successfully.","affected_stakeholders":["Product Management","Finance Team","Operations Team","Customers","Customer Support"],"options":["A: Change invoicing timing — Operations invoices at shipment instead of placement, opening a cancellation window between placement and shipment. Requires Operations to find another mechanism to lock pricing (e.g., price snapshots).","B: Introduce credit notes — Finance distinguishes between 'cancelling an invoice' (forbidden) and 'issuing a credit note against an invoice' (allowed). Cancellation triggers a credit note rather than voiding the invoice. Invoice remains on the ledger but is financially neutralized.","C: Introduce proforma invoices — Operations issues a non-binding proforma at placement for price-locking. The legal invoice is generated at shipment. Proforma can be voided freely, enabling cancellation.","D: Restrict PM's requirement — Accept that cancellation is only possible in the brief window before invoicing completes (effectively never, given synchronous invoicing at placement)."],"recommendation":"Option B (credit notes) is the industry-standard resolution. It preserves Finance's ledger integrity (invoices are never deleted/voided), satisfies Operations' price-locking need (invoice is issued at placement), and enables the PM's cancellation feature (credit note reverses the financial effect). This is how most ERP and order management systems handle this exact conflict."}],"rejection_reasons":[{"violation":"The three requirements as stated are mutually exclusive and cannot be implemented together.","business_rule_broken":"BR-1 is rendered impossible by the combination of BR-2 and BR-3. Every order is invoiced at creation (BR-3), and invoiced orders cannot be cancelled (BR-2), therefore no order can ever be cancelled — directly contradicting BR-1."}]}
